summary: The app writes HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Multimedia\Audio\DeviceCpl\ShowHiddenDevices = 1 to expose hidden audio devices in the classic Sound control panel. The repo preserves a normalized Procmon summary from the 2026-03-14 capture showing rundll32.exe querying this exact value with both Data:1 and Data:0, while the checked-in Procmon markdown files are placeholders because the raw PML is off-git. A Ghidra headless pass on 2026-03-26 against mmsys.cpl decompiled the corresponding SHGetValueW and SHSetValueW handlers, and a v3.1 runtime lane on 2026-03-27 cleanly applied, launched the Sound control panel, and restored the value on Win25H2Clean. That keeps the record aligned with the current v3.1 cross-layer contract even though no primary Microsoft documentation page for the DeviceCpl contract was captured.

setting.name: Show Hidden Audio Devices
setting.casual_explanation: This makes hidden audio devices appear in the classic Sound control panel. It is mainly useful for troubleshooting, advanced audio setup, or driver cleanup.
setting.why_people_change_it: People enable it when they need to inspect or troubleshoot devices that the normal sound list does not show.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Showing hidden devices can help debugging, but it can also expose stale or confusing audio entries that most users never need to see. This is a runtime-observed control-panel preference rather than a Microsoft-documented policy surface.
setting.professional_notes: The app writes ShowHiddenDevices = 1 under HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Multimedia\Audio\DeviceCpl. The checked-in Procmon markdown files for the 2026-03-14 capture are placeholders because the raw PML is off-git, but the record still preserves a normalized hit summary showing rundll32.exe launching shell32.dll,Control_RunDLL mmsys.cpl,,0 and issuing RegQueryValue reads for ShowHiddenDevices with Data:1 and Data:0. On 2026-03-26, a Ghidra headless pass against mmsys.cpl decompiled the DeviceCpl flag handlers and showed SHGetValueW and SHSetValueW calls for ShowHiddenDevices on the same branch. On 2026-03-27, the v3.1 runtime lane cleanly applied the value, launched the Sound control panel, and restored the original missing state on Win25H2Clean. That is enough to keep the record validated as a runtime-observed preference under the current v3.1 cross-layer rule, but not as a fully documented Microsoft contract.

allowed_values.label: Hide hidden audio devices
allowed_values.meaning: Procmon confirmed that the classic Sound control panel reads ShowHiddenDevices = 0 on this build. This is the observed off state for the preference.

allowed_values.label: Show hidden audio devices
allowed_values.meaning: Procmon confirmed that the classic Sound control panel reads ShowHiddenDevices = 1 on this build. This is the observed show-hidden state used by the app.

targets.notes: Validated as a runtime-observed control-panel preference. The absent-value default was not captured in this pass.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes ShowHiddenDevices = 1, and Procmon confirmed that the classic Sound control panel reads the same value as a live preference on this build.

windows_defaults.label: Default state not captured
windows_defaults.applies_to: Classic Sound control panel profiles where the value is absent

states.rationale: This pass validated the 0 and 1 states through Procmon, but it did not capture the absent-value default.

windows_defaults.notes: Use this record as a validated runtime preference map for 0 and 1, not as a complete Microsoft-documented default model.

recommended_profiles.label: Show hidden audio devices
avoid_for: General users who do not need extra device visibility

states.rationale: Set the runtime-observed preference to the show-hidden state that the Sound control panel reads on this build.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You need the classic Sound control panel to expose hidden devices.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want the cleanest possible device list with fewer stale entries.
tradeoffs: Hidden devices become visible in the classic Sound control panel.
The device list can become noisier or more confusing.

evidence.title: Procmon capture - Sound control panel ShowHiddenDevices runtime reads
evidence.summary: The raw Procmon PML is off-git, but the repo preserves a normalized summary of the 2026-03-14 capture showing rundll32.exe launching shell32.dll,Control_RunDLL mmsys.cpl,,0 and querying HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Multimedia\Audio\DeviceCpl\ShowHiddenDevices with Data:1 and Data:0 in separate reversible passes. The checked-in Procmon markdown files are placeholders for those off-git captures.

evidence.title: Our Ghidra decompilation - mmsys.cpl DeviceCpl flag handlers
evidence.summary: A headless Ghidra pass on mmsys.cpl decompiled the functions that call SHGetValueW and SHSetValueW for HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Multimedia\Audio\DeviceCpl\ShowHiddenDevices and ShowDisconnectedDevices. That gives direct code-side proof that the classic Sound control panel reads and writes these DeviceCpl flags.

decision.why: The record keeps Class A under the current v3.1 matrix because the repo still preserves converged runtime and static proof: a normalized Procmon summary from the 2026-03-14 Sound control panel capture shows rundll32.exe querying ShowHiddenDevices with both 1 and 0, the checked-in Ghidra export decompiles the mmsys.cpl handlers that read and write the same DeviceCpl flag, the 2026-03-27 runtime lane cleanly applied and restored the value on Win25H2Clean, and the app writes the same show-hidden state with a clean restore story. This remains a runtime-observed preference rather than a Microsoft-documented contract.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: audio_devicecpl_query_20260314.pml: rundll32.exe RegQueryValue HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Multimedia\Audio\DeviceCpl\ShowHiddenDevices Data:1. audio_devicecpl_query_zero_20260314.pml: rundll32.exe RegQueryValue HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Multimedia\Audio\DeviceCpl\ShowHiddenDevices Data:0.
validation_proof.notes: This v3.1 re-audit keeps the normalized Procmon hit summary in a record-scoped note because the checked-in Procmon markdown files are only placeholders for the off-git raw PML. The same note also ties the runtime summary and Ghidra export back to the record.
